docs.title: Introduction
AI and Windows Granite Workshop
docs.text:
Welcome to our workshop! In this workshop we'll be using the open-sourced IBM Granite AI foundation models for a number of use cases that demonstrates the value of generative AI.

The goals of this workshop are:

  • Installing Open Source AI tools on your own Windows 11 laptop (Mac instructions to follow soon) to run AI locally.
  • Running LLM models via command line
  • Running LLM models with a UI interface
  • Try to improve results with Retrieval-Augmented Generation
  • Running with a multimodal LLM

docs.title: About this workshop
docs.text:
The introductory page of the workshop is broken down into the following sections:

  • Agenda
  • Compatibility
  • Technology Used
  • Credits

docs.title: Agenda
docs.text: Pre-Work Prerequisites for the workshop Lab 1: Command-Line Ollama Lab 1 Lab 2: Using Open-WebUI Interface Lab 2 Lab 3: Retrieval-Augmented Generation Lab 3 Lab 4: Graphics and MultiModal LLM Lab 4

docs.title: Compatibility
docs.text:
This workshop has been tested on the following platforms:

  • Windows 11 (Ideally with at least 16GB of memory, but 8GB memory can work with smaller models.
  • (MacOS instructions to follow soon)

docs.title: Technology Used
docs.text:
• IBM Granite Models
  • Open Source Ollama
  • Open source Open-WebUI

docs.title: Credits
docs.text:
• James Busche
  • Yi-Hong Wang

docs.title: Command-Line Ollama
1.1 List the available models downloaded
docs.text: ollama list

docs.title: 1.2 Run one of the models, for example:
docs.text: ollama run granite3.3:2b

docs.title: 1.3 Issue it some questions, try it out.  For example:
docs.text:
What is the difference between a mammal and a reptile? Provide examples for each.

Create an easy to make chocolate chip cookie recipe

Who was Marie Curie and what are her key discoveries in the field of chemistry and physics?

docs.title: 1.4 Clear the context
docs.text: /clear

docs.title: 1.5 Give it clear instructions to change it's behavior.  For example:
docs.text:
Answer questions from now on in Spanish

Answer questions like a pirate

docs.title: 1.6 Exit out of the current model
docs.text: /bye

docs.title: 1.7 List and then start another model:
docs.text:
ollama list

ollama run granite3.2-vision:latest

docs.title: 1.8 Issue it some questions, try it out.  For example:
docs.text:
Create an easy to make chocolate chip cookie recipe

List the ingredients as well as 5 optional additions

docs.title: 1.9  Exit out of the current model:
docs.text: /bye

docs.title: Using Open-WebUI Interface
2.1 Activate your python virtual environment if you haven't already:
docs.text:
WindowsMacOS
  1. Activate your python virtual environment: 
    "venv/Scripts/activate.bat"
  1. Activate your python virtual environment: 
    source venv/bin/activate

docs.title: 2.2 Start the Open WebUI
docs.text: open-webui serve

docs.title: 2.3 Hit the localhost with the browser:
docs.text: http://localhost:8080

docs.title: 2.4 Pick a model to use, for example:
docs.text: granite3.3:8b

docs.title: 2.5 Issue it some questions, try it out.  For example:
docs.text:
Describe the water cycle in detail, mentioning evaporation, condensation, precipitation, and collection.

Who was Ada Lovelace, and why is she considered a pioneer in computer science?

Why are cats better companions than dogs?

explain your reasoning for why cats and dogs are not inherently better

docs.title: 2.6 Clear the context by clicking on "New Chat"
2.7 Give it clear instructions to change the behavior.  For example:
docs.text: Only answer questions in rhyme. What was domesticated first, a dog or a cat?

docs.title: 2.8 Clear the context by clicking on "New Chat"
Retrieval-Augmented Generation
3.1 In the Open-WebUI, switch to the smallest granite model:
docs.text: granite3.3:2b

docs.title: 3.2 Click on "New Chat" so the context is clear
3.3 Ask it for:
docs.text:
List the past and current CEOs of the IBM corporation from first to last.
The answer isn't correct.

docs.title: 3.4 Click on "New Chat" to clear the context
3.5 Download a small text file with the correct list of IBM CEOs to your Downloads folder:
docs.text: IBM.txt

docs.title: 3.6 Click on the "+" and then Upload files
docs.text: Upload the IBM.txt file that you just downloaded

docs.title: 3.7 Now ask it our question about the CEOs of IBM:
docs.text:
List all the past and current CEOs of the IBM corporation from first to last.
The answer should now be correct. (For example, always before it forgets Jon Akers)

docs.title: 3.8 We can also find and download information to pdf from Wikipedia:
docs.text: For example: History of IBM

docs.title: 3.9  On the right, click on "Tools" and Click on "Download as PDF"
3.10 Then use this History_of_IBM.pdf as a RAG by clicking on the + and adding "History_of_IBM.pdf" as a file.
3.11 Then use the Open-WebUI to ask more questions about IBM.
docs.text:
Who is the first female CEO of IBM?
The answer should be Ginni Rometty

docs.title: Graphics and MultiModal LLM
4.1 Activate your python virtual environment if you haven't already:
docs.text:
WindowsMacOS
  1. Activate your python virtual environment: 
    "venv/Scripts/activate.bat"
  1. Activate your python virtual environment: 
    source venv/bin/activate

docs.title: 4.2 Start the Open WebUI
docs.text: open-webui serve

docs.title: 4.3 Hit the localhost with the browser:
docs.text: http://localhost:8080

docs.title: 4.4 Pick the vision model to use, for example:
docs.text: granite3.2-vision:2b

docs.title: 4.5 This small vision model is multi-modal.
docs.text:
Multi-modal means the model has been trained from multiple modalities of data, such as text, images, audio, and video. So while the primary use of the vision model is to understand visual content, it's also trained with language datasets. So you can issue it questions and commands and it will still respond as a small language model.

For example, you can ask a few simple questions: 

Why would hydrogen gas not be preferred in balloons?

Create an easy apple pie recipe for the upcoming holiday?

docs.title: 4.6 Clear the context by clicking on "New Chat"
4.7 Using another tab on the browser, search for a image.  For example:
docs.text: Kangaroo

docs.title: 4.8 In the search results, click on the "Images" tab and right-click on one of the photos you like and select "Copy Image"
4.9 Using the Open-WebUI browser, do a CTRL-V to paste the copied image to the Open-WebUI and press enter. The Granite vision model will summarize what it sees from the image.
4.10 Go ahead and ask the AI more questions about the animal in the image.  For example:
docs.text: What is the lifespan for the animal in the photo?

docs.title: 4.11 Keep learning!
docs.text:
If you have additional time and interest, you can continue to try out what you've learned about using Open Source AI tools to run your own local AI system.

Have fun!

docs.title: Pre-work
docs.text:
Assumptions:

These labs can be run on a Windows 11 or MacOS machine. Click on the operation system you are using below to get the corresponding instructions.

docs.title: Ollama Installation
docs.text:
WindowsMacOS

  1. Download Ollama for Windows

  2. Install Ollama by double-clicking on the downloaded OllamaSetup.exe And then clicking on "Install"

  1. Use Homebrew to install Ollama: 

    brew install ollama

  2. Start the Ollama server. You will leave this running during the workshop. 

    ollama serve

docs.title: Download Granite models
docs.text:
For the workshop, you will need to download at least 3 of the granite models: 

ollama pull granite3.3:2b
ollama pull granite3.2-vision:2b
ollama pull granite3.3:8b
Note: We won't use them for this lab, but if you wanted to try some other LLM models, you can start with: 
ollama pull deepseek-r1:1.5b
ollama pull llama3.2:3b
Check out the entire Hugging face library of models at: https://ollama.com/library

docs.title: Install Python
docs.text:
For the workshop you will need Python 3.11.9. Follow the steps below for your operating system:

WindowsMacOS

  1. Download the Python python-3.11.9-amd64.exe

  2. Double-click to install it

    Note

    While installing - make sure you check the box to "add the python.exe to path"**

  3. Close and re-open your command prompt and then check to make sure python is findable on the path by typing: 

    python --version
    It should return: "Python 3.11.9"

Note

If it doesn't find the python command, likely you forgot to check the box to put python in the path. No worry, just:

  1. Double-click on the downloaded python-3.1.9-amd64.exe file again and this time chose "Modify" and click "next"
  2. Put a check next to the box: "Add Python to the environnment variables" and click "Install".

  1. Optionally, you can also download and install git from git-scm.com/downloads

    1. The latest one as of writing this document is: Git-2.49.0-64-bit.exe
    2. Double-click on the downloaded Git-2.49.0-64-bit.exe, take all the defaults and install
  1. Install pyenv and git for MacOS. If you don't have them already: 
    brew update
brew install pyenv git

  2. Install Python 3.11.9 for MacOS 

    pyenv install 3.11.9

  3. Use the Python version you just installed: 

    pyenv global 3.11.9

  4. Verify the Python version: 

    pyenv version-name
    It should return now 3.11.9

docs.title: Python Virtual Environment:
docs.text:
Python virtual environment is used to isolated Python installations. Let's create one called "venv" and use it for the workshop.

WindowsMacOS

  1. Open the command prompt run the following command to create a new virtual environment: 

    python -m venv --upgrade-deps --clear venv

  2. Activate your python environment by typing: 

    "venv/Scripts/activate.bat"

  1. Open the terminal run the following command to create a new virtual environment: 

    python3 -m venv --upgrade-deps --clear venv

  2. Activate the virtual environment: 

    source venv/bin/activate

docs.title: Install the Open-WebUI
docs.text:
1. Install the Open-WebUI by using the pip command: 

    pip install open-webui

  2. Start up open-webui: 

    open-webui serve

docs.title: Access Open-WebUI
docs.text:
1. Once the open-webui starts, use a browser to login to open-webui: http://localhost:8080

  2. Enter a name, email address and password - these are stored locally.

docs.title: Cleaning up after the lab is complete:
docs.text:
1. Remove all the models you don't want: 

    ollama list
ollama rm granite3.3:2b granite3.3:8b granite3.2-vision:2b

  2. Uninstall ollama:

    WindowsMacOS

    In the search bar, search for Settings → Apps → Installed Apps and search for Ollama and click on Uninstall

    In a terminal, use brew to uninstall ollama: 

    brew uninstall ollama

  3. Stop and remove open-webUI:

    WindowsMacOS

    1. In the command line where open-webui is running, do a ctrl-C to stop the "open-webui serve"

    2. Deactivate your python environment session by typing: 

      venv/Scripts/deactivate.bat

    3. If you want to remove the virutal environment completely: 

      rmdir /s venv

    1. In the command line where open-webui is running, do a ctrl-C to stop the "open-webui serve"
    2. Deactivate your python environment session by typing: 
      deactivate
    3. If you want to remove the virutal environment completely: 
      rm -rf venv

docs.title: Prerequisite
docs.text:
This workshop uses ......

Please follow the installation links below to set up your working environment:

  • prerequisite 1
  • prerequisite 2

docs.title: Clone the workshop repository
docs.text:
Various parts of this workshop will require the configuration files from the workshop repository. Use the following command to download the whole repository 

git clone https://github.com/IBM/intro-ai-llm-workshop.git
cd intro-ai-llm-workshop

Or download the repository as a zip file, unzip it and get into the intro-ai-llm-workshop-main directory.
